content.full_text:
August 26, 1976

76-08-203

Mr. William Coats
Souza, Coats and McInnis
P.O. Box 1129
Tracy, California 95376

Dear Mr. Coats:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning a potential conflict of interest between your private
law practice and your duties as City Attorney for the City of
Tracy. Specifically, the request concerns your role as executor
of an estate, the assets of which include a cab company, and
your role as legal advisor to the city regarding the purchase of
that cab company. You ask whether you "have a sufficient
interest in the property or substantial indirect participation
in the actions of the [City] Council" such as to constitute a
Conflict of Interest under the Political Reform Act. It is the
opinion of the staff that your participation in the city's
purchase of the cab company would be a conflict of interest.

Government Code §87100 contains the general prohibition
against decision-making in a Conflict of Interest situation;
that is, no public official shall participate in making
decisions in which he or she has a financial interest. The
Fair Political Practices Commission has further defined
"participates in the making of a governmental decision" in 2
California Administrative Code §18700(c), as amended on August
5, 1976, and this regulation is instructive in determining whether
you, as City Attorney, in fact, "participate" in the City
Council's deliberations.

This regulation provides, in pertinent part, that a public
official participates in decision-making when he or she:

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-
maker, either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by:

(b) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the official...
and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

-423

8-26-76

Even though the City Council must ultimately approve the purchase and you have no vote on the matter, your remittance of legal advice regarding the purchase or review of the purchase contract, as City Attorney, brings your participation within the purview of this regulation. Thus, since you participate in the making of the decision within the meaning of the Act, you must disqualify yourself from the decision-making process if you have a financial interest in the decision.

An official has a financial interest in a decision when "it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally", on:

(c) Any source of income ... aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made ... (Government Code §87103)

According to information supplied by you, the sale of this cab company would materially affect the estate and would generate executor's fees amounting to $700-800. Thus a decision by the City Council to purchase the company will affect a source of income to you in a financially material way. You have a financial interest in the decision within the meaning of Government Code §§87100 and 87103.

The execution of the bill of sale after court approval in your capacity as executor, is not a governmental decision and presents no Conflict of Interest under the Political Reform Act.

If you have any further questions, please contact Delbert Spurlock of the Conflicts of Interest Division at 916/322-6444.

Sincerely,

Sharon Stevens
Conflicts of Interest
Division

SS:jhm

.I

LAW OFFICES OF

SOUZA, COATS & McINNIS

ALFRED A. SOUZA (1911-1975)
WILLIAM COATS
WALTER J. McINNIS

1011 PARKER AVENUE
P. O. BOX 1129
TRACY, CALIFORNIA 95376

TELEPHONE
935-3222
AREA CODE 209

July 29, 1976

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA., 95804

ATTN: Michael Bennett, Executive Director

Gentlemen:

I am a part time City Attorney for the City of Tracy. I also have a
general law practice.

I have been named as the Executor of the estate of the individual who owned
and operated the cab company within the Tracy area, including the City of
Tracy. I have no beneficial interest in the estate. The City desires to
acquire the cab business, including all of its assets. I would execute the
Bill of Sale after court approval.

The City Attorney acts as the legal adviser to the Council and City staff.
I do not have any vote on any of the issues which are decided by the Council.
If the City acquires the cab company it will be necessary for the City
Council to approve the purchase.

Under these circumstances, in your opinion, do I have a sufficient interest
in the property or substantial indirect participation in the actions of the
Council as to constitute a conflict of interest under the Fair Political
Practices Act.

I should appreciate your informal letter opinion on this matter. Thank you.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM COATS

WC/ps

I have been named as the Executor of the estate of the individual who owned and operated the cab company within the Tracy area, including one [serving] City of Tracy. The City Attorney acts as the legal adviser to the Council and City Staffs. I do not have any vote on any of the issues which are decided by the Council. If the city acquires the cab company it will be necessary for the city Council to approve the purchase. Under these circumstances, in your opinion, do I have a sufficient interest in the property or substantial indirect participation in the actions of the Council as to constitute a conflict of interest under the Fair Political Practices Act.

Even though the city council must ultimately approve the purchase and you have no vote on the matter, your rendition of legal advice regarding the purchase or review of the purchase contract, as City Attorney, brings your participation within the purview of this regulation. Thus, since you participate in the making of the decision within the meaning of the Act, you must disqualify yourself from the decision-making process if you have a financial interest in the decision. According to information supplied by you, the sale of this cab company would materially effect the estate and would generate executor's fees amounting to $700-900. Thus, a decision by the city council to purchase the company will affect a source of income to you in a financially material way. You have a financial interest in the decision within the meaning of Government code §87100 and §7103.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city attorney who is also the executor of an estate containing a cab company, and who would provide legal advice regarding the city council's potential purchase of that cab company, has a sufficient financial interest or participatory role to constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the requester has a conflict of interest and must disqualify himself from participating in the city council's decision to purchase the cab company because: (1) his legal advice to the city on the purchase constitutes participation in the decision-making process, and (2) he has a material financial interest in the outcome through executor's fees of $700-900. The execution of the bill of sale after court approval in his capacity as executor is not itself a governmental decision and presents no conflict.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (July 29, 1975) and FPPC response (August 26, 1976). OCR quality is moderate with some character recognition errors but content is reasonably clear. The question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable and extracted verbatim from the FPPC response letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I am a part time city Attorney for the city of Tracy. I also have a general law practice. I have been named as the Executor of the estate of the individual who owned and operated the cab company within the Tracy area, including one [serving] City of Tracy. I have no beneficial interest in the estate. The city desires to acquire the cab business, including all of its assets. I would execute the Bill of sale after court approval. The City Attorney acts as the legal adviser to the Council and City Staffs. I do not have any vote on any of the issues which are decided by the Council. If the city acquires the cab company it will be necessary for the city Council to approve the purchase. Under these circumstances, in your opinion, do I have a sufficient interest in the property or substantial indirect participation in the actions of the Council as to constitute a conflict of interest under the Fair Political Practices Act.

CONCLUSION: It is the opinion of the staff that your participation in the city's purchase of the cab company would be a conflict of interest. Even though the city council must ultimately approve the purchase and you have no vote on the matter, your rendition of legal advice regarding the purchase or review of the purchase contract, as City Attorney, brings your participation within the purview of this regulation. Thus, since you participate in the making of the decision within the meaning of the Act, you must disqualify yourself from the decision-making process if you have a financial interest in the decision. According to information supplied by you, the sale of this cab company would materially effect the estate and would generate executor's fees amounting to $700-900. Thus, a decision by the city council to purchase the company will affect a source of income to you in a financially material way. You have a financial interest in the decision within the meaning of Government code §87100 and §7103.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a city attorney's inquiry about whether serving as both legal adviser to the city council on a potential purchase of a cab company and as executor of an estate containing that company creates a conflict of interest. The FPPC concludes that a conflict exists and the city attorney must disqualify himself from advising on the purchase decision.
